1: Oh, that you would tear open the heavens and come down — the climax of the lament that began at Isaiah 63:7. The tearing-open is a plea for theophany more dramatic than Sinai: mountains quaking at YHWH's presence. The mountains quaking at YHWH's coming recurs in Micah 1:4, Nahum 1:5, and Habakkuk 3:6 — it is the standard register for YHWH's war-presence. The prayer is for YHWH to make his nearness unmistakable and terrifying.
2: As when fire kindles brushwood and water boils — to make your name known (yada, disp 2) to your enemies, so that the nations would tremble at your presence. The fire-water images are Sinai and the Red Sea: YHWH's fire on the mountain and his control of water in the exodus. The prayer names both as the precedent for the present petition. The goal is not destruction for its own sake but the knowing of YHWH's name by the nations — the same goal as the exodus (Exodus 9:16).
3: When you did awe-inspiring things we did not expect, you came down — the mountains quaked at your presence. The Sinai theophany is the paradigm case: Israel did not request or anticipate YHWH's self-disclosure in smoke and fire and earthquake. The awe-inspiring-things-we-did-not-expect pattern establishes that YHWH's greatest acts exceed human prediction.
4: From of old (olam, disp 3) no one has heard, no ear has perceived, no eye has seen any God besides you, who acts on behalf of those who wait for him. The incomparability of YHWH: no other god acts for those who wait. This verse is quoted in 1 Corinthians 2:9 as the pattern of what the Spirit reveals — what eye has not seen, ear has not heard. The scope of YHWH's acting for-those-who-wait goes beyond what natural perception can anticipate, which is why the Spirit must reveal it.
5: You come to the aid of those who joyfully do right, those who remember you in your ways. But you were angry, and we sinned; in these ways we were and have been for a long time (olam, disp 3). The confession is embedded in the acknowledgment of YHWH's prior faithfulness: YHWH aids those who do right, but we have not done right. The olam-sinning (sinning for a long time) makes the confession generational, not merely personal.
6: We have all become like one who is unclean; all our righteous deeds (tsedaqot, tsedaqah disp 3) are like a filthy rag; we all wither like a leaf, and our iniquities, like the wind, carry us away. The most explicit confession in the Isaiah corpus: even the community's tsedaqah-acts are contaminated. The filthy-rag image is deliberately shocking — the very acts that should commend them to YHWH are themselves impure. The ruach (wind/spirit, disp 4) that carries them away here is not the Spirit of YHWH but their own iniquities functioning as a wind — the inversion of the Spirit who guided Israel (Isaiah 63:14).
7: There is no one who calls on your name, who stirs himself to take hold of you; for you have hidden your face from us and delivered us into the hand of our iniquity. The mutual abandonment: no one seeks YHWH; YHWH has hidden his face. The hidden-face is the covenant curse (Deuteronomy 31:17-18; Psalms 88:14). But the phrase delivered us into the hand of our iniquity makes iniquity itself the captor — a striking personification. Sin is not just a wrong act but a power that holds.
8: But now, YHWH (YHWH, disp 4), you are our Father; we are the clay, and you are our potter; we are all the work of your hands. The potter-clay image (Jeremiah 18:1-10; Romans 9:20-21) asserts that the relationship between YHWH and Israel is not a covenant of equals but of creator and creature — which is precisely why the community can appeal to YHWH's creative sovereignty to reconstitute them. The appeal is: since you made us, you can remake us.
9: Do not be exceedingly angry, YHWH (YHWH, disp 4); do not remember iniquity forever. Please look — we are all your people. The petition invokes covenant identity: your people. The not-remembering-iniquity-forever appeal to YHWH's own promise: Isaiah 43:25 (I am the one who blots out your transgressions for my own sake and will not remember your sins).
10: Your holy (qodesh, disp 2) cities have become a wasteland; Zion is a wilderness, Jerusalem a desolation. The triple description of destruction — wasteland, wilderness, desolation — grounds the prayer in the concrete reality of exile. The holy cities (plural) include not only Jerusalem but the towns of Judah, all designated as YHWH's sacred precincts that have been violated.
11: Our holy (qodesh, disp 2) and glorious house where our fathers praised you has been burned with fire, and all our cherished places have become ruins. The temple's destruction is the focal point of the lament: the house built for YHWH's glory (kabod) is ash. The loss is not merely structural but theological — the place of YHWH's dwelling on earth is gone. Will you hold yourself back (v 12)?
12: Will you hold yourself back for all this, YHWH (YHWH, disp 4)? Will you keep silent while you afflict us so grievously? The final petition is purely interrogative — the prayer ends not with confident declaration but with pressing the question. The silence of YHWH in the face of Zion's ruin is itself the thing being challenged. The chapter that began with the tearing of the heavens ends with the question of whether YHWH will remain silent — which sets up chapter 65's answer: YHWH has not been silent; Israel has not listened.
